original_text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose salary ranges in job postings.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose only whether a role is hourly or salaried in job postings.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose a broad pay category, such as 'competitive salary,' in job postings.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose a general salary range to candidates only upon request during the hiring process.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose salary ranges in job postings.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose salary ranges along with a summary of benefits and bonus eligibility in job postings.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose full compensation details—including salary ranges, bonus structures, equity grants, and benefits valuations—in job postings.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose full compensation details—including salary ranges, bonus structures, equity grants, benefits valuations, pay equity statistics, and advancement criteria—in job postings.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the depth and richness of disclosure within the same federal-law framework. Tier 1 discloses almost nothing useful; Tier 4 matches the original salary-range requirement; Tiers 5–7 progressively add more meaningful compensation and equity transparency. The distinctions are in scope and depth of information, not in the number of separate laws or unrelated items.

preference_direction: Greater comprehensiveness of disclosure gives job seekers more complete and actionable information about compensation, reducing information asymmetry and enabling fairer negotiations—making higher tiers strictly more preferable.
